summary: DeVault argues that the Commons Clause, an addendum to free software licenses that prohibits selling the software, creates nonfree franken-licenses while deliberately hijacking trusted open source terminology. He criticizes projects like Redis Labs for falsely describing Commons Clause software as 'open source' or 'permissive,' calling this practice dishonest and unethical. The piece emphasizes that 'free software' and 'open source' have precise, well-established definitions that cannot be co-opted for marketing purposes. He concludes that while anyone can choose nonfree licensing, they must be honest about it rather than claiming community benefits they haven't earned.
key_points: The Commons Clause deliberately hijacks trusted terminology like 'Apache' and 'Commons' to make nonfree software appear open source
Free software and open source have unambiguous definitions from GNU and OSI that Commons Clause software fails to meet
Redis Labs actively listed Commons Clause modules alongside OSI-approved software and ignored community complaints
The author doesn't oppose nonfree software per se, but demands honesty about licensing rather than false claims of openness
Dgraph is praised as a positive example for rolling back Commons Clause and returning to genuine Apache 2.0
money_quotes: The Commons Clause serves to remove software from the commons.
These definitions are so basic, important and well-respected that dismissing them is akin to dismissing climate change.
Claiming your software is open source, permissively licensed, free software, etc, when you use the Commons Clause, is lying.
We're not going to forget about shady, dishonest, and unethical companies like Redis Labs.

I'm not going to sit here and tell you not to make nonfree software. You have every right to license your work in any way you choose. However, if you choose not to use a FOSS license, you need to own up to it.
There are many benefits to being a member of the free software community, but you are not entitled to them if your software isn't.
Instead of being a new software license, it tries to stick itself onto other respected licences.
key_insight: Misrepresenting nonfree software as open source is not a gray area or marketing choice — it is a lie that undermines the precise definitions protecting the entire free software community.
